scenario: A growing team writes more BPMN diagrams. The team lead wants to establish naming conventions -- process IDs, task names, event names -- so diagrams are consistent and readable. They wonder what conventions are recommended.
question: What BPMN naming convention best practices apply?

options.text: " / "yes" / "no"); technical element IDs using PascalCase with type prefix (Task_ReviewTweet, Gateway_TweetApproved). These are team-standard recommendations, not engine-enforced rules. Edit IDs in Modeler's properties panel.

Complete freedom for modellers to pick any naming style without conventions is an anti-pattern in team environments. Without agreed conventions, different team members will produce diagrams with inconsistent task names, element IDs, and event labels. This inconsistency makes diagrams harder to read, makes process ID-based API calls unpredictable, and complicates migration planning. Camunda provides official naming best practices documentation precisely to help teams establish consistent, readable, and maintainable naming conventions.

Camunda's BPMN engine does not enforce specific naming conventions -- the engine accepts any valid XML identifiers and labels. md are official recommendations, not technical requirements enforced at deployment or runtime. Teams can choose to adopt Camunda's recommended conventions or establish their own, but the engine will execute the process regardless of naming style.

Using English for all BPMN identifiers is a common industry practice because it aligns with technical tooling conventions and makes diagrams accessible to international team members. Many teams in non-English-speaking regions successfully model processes using their local language for labels and display names while keeping technical IDs in a consistent ASCII-friendly format.

a.text: Correct. BPMN best practices for naming align around clarity and consistency: Process IDs as stable machine-friendly identifiers (used in deploy / API calls / migration plans). Lowercase, hyphens or underscores, no spaces. Process Names as human-readable display. Tasks as actions (verb-object): "Validate Order" not just "Order." Events as states / occurrences (noun): "Payment Received," not "Receive Payment." Gateways as questions: "Approved?" or "Inventory available?" -- readers immediately know it's a decision point. Beyond naming: arrow direction conventions (left-to-right or top-to-bottom), consistent use of pools/lanes, modular subprocesses, and clear documentation comments all improve diagram quality. Camunda's Best Practices documentation captures many of these conventions.

d.text: Incorrect. English common but not enforced.
